title: Meta’s smartglasses mean any child can be covertly filmed. In the age of AI, how do we tackle that risk?
excerpt: The company argues that it’s for individuals to ensure they don’t ‘actively exploit’ this technology. That won’t keep children safe, says Guardian columnist Gaby Hinsliff
content_html:
It took me a while to realise what the man sitting next to me on the train was doing. He was flicking through pictures of a little girl on his phone; zooming in on some, cropping the images. Hardly unusual, of course, except that on closer inspection the pictures were very clearly of the toddler sitting opposite us, playing with her parents. On a crowded train of people mostly staring obliviously at our own phones, a stranger had been covertly photographing that little girl over and over again. And if it hadn’t been for that very visible phone screen giving him away, he wouldn’t have been caught.

That encounter was a while ago but my memory was jogged last week listening to a clip of Meta’s VP of wearables, Alex Himel, talk fondly to the BBC about using his new smartglasses to film his daughter singing in a talent contest. They let him capture the memory without having to watch her through a screen, he said, freeing him to be “kind of living in the moment, head up and hands free”. So far, so sweet. But what about the risk of creeps potentially using them to film other people’s daughters covertly, heads up and hands free? Meta’s smartglasses have a tiny flashing warning light on the frame to indicate when the wearer is recording, but the technology is still new enough that many people don’t think to look for it. And in the age of AI, stolen images of children matter. Taken in real life or scraped from the internet, they’re the raw material for generating realistic-looking child abuse material of the most extreme and disturbing kind. (If that sounds like a grim but ultimately fairly victimless crime, the Internet Watch Foundation warned in a recent report that AI-generated material risks fuelling sexual interest in children, normalising violence and increasing the risk of real-life offending.)

When Google launched the first smartglasses in 2013, they didn’t catch on: too nerdy-looking but also potentially too creepy, given the possibilities they afforded stalkers, trolls, criminals gathering intelligence and law enforcement in repressive regimes. But big tech hasn’t given up on the dream of connecting humans more seamlessly to the internet, and newer versions learn from past mistakes. Meta’s are cooler, featuring collaborations with Ray-Ban and Kylie Jenner, and are pitched unashamedly at parents under the wholesome slogan: “never miss a moment”. Want to capture your baby’s milestones, or do all the things you surreptitiously do when you’re with the kids – checking emails, shopping online, listening to podcasts – without taking your eyes off them? It’s an appealing sell to busy, guilty, multi-tasking parents. But the safeguarding concerns around covert filming are real enough for many nurseries and schools to have started banning them from the premises.

A twist in the tale, however, is that making them socially unacceptable risks creating casualties of its own: the technology is potentially life-changing for blind people, helping them navigate confidently around obstacles or instantly translate text to audio.

Meta has argued that it is for individuals to ensure they don’t “actively exploit” the technology, which makes you wonder if it learned anything at all from watching humans get to grips with its social media platforms. Regulatory options might include new privacy legislation designed for the digital age, forcing manufacturers to make it far clearer when the glasses are recording – maybe even with an audible warning, like the beep of a reversing lorry – at least until we’re all primed to look twice at anyone in specs. But since that would make them insufferably embarrassing to wear, it’s unlikely to please tech companies seeking a lucrative mass market.

Another option could be holding manufacturers, not wearers, liable for malicious usage – though that could mean years of painfully slow litigation, concluding long after the damage is done. The nuclear option, meanwhile, would be a before-market regulatory regime for novel technology recognising its capacity for social harm, much as we have for clearing new pharmaceutical drugs or novel foodstuffs.

The best option is arguably the one history suggests is least likely to happen: for a myopic tech industry to start better anticipating, and designing out, potential harms that should be visible from space. There are some things, surely, that nobody needs smartglasses to see.

  • Gaby Hinsliff is a Guardian columnist

sentences.zh: 我花了一段时间才意识到火车上坐在我旁边的那个人在做什么。

sentences.en: He was flicking through pictures of a little girl on his phone; zooming in on some, cropping the images.
sentences.zh: 他正在手机上翻看一个小女孩的照片；放大一些图像，裁剪图像。

sentences.en: Hardly unusual, of course, except that on closer inspection the pictures were very clearly of the toddler sitting opposite us, playing with her parents.
sentences.zh: 当然，这并不罕见，只是仔细观察，这些照片非常清楚地显示那个小孩坐在我们对面，和她的父母一起玩耍。

sentences.en: On a crowded train of people mostly staring obliviously at our own phones, a stranger had been covertly photographing that little girl over and over again.
sentences.zh: 在拥挤的火车上，大多数人都在不知不觉地盯着自己的手机，一个陌生人偷偷地一遍又一遍地拍摄那个小女孩。

sentences.en: And if it hadn’t been for that very visible phone screen giving him away, he wouldn’t have been caught.
sentences.zh: 如果不是那个非常明显的手机屏幕暴露了他的身份，他就不会被抓住。

Meta’s smartglasses have a tiny flashing warning light on the frame to indicate when the wearer is recording, but the technology is still new enough that many people don’t think to look for it. Taken in real life or scraped from the internet, they’re the raw material for generating realistic-looking child abuse material of the most extreme and disturbing kind. (If that sounds like a grim but ultimately fairly victimless crime, the Internet Watch Foundation warned in a recent report that AI-generated material risks fuelling sexual interest in children, normalising violence and increasing the risk of real-life offending.)

sentences.en: That encounter was a while ago but my memory was jogged last week listening to a clip of Meta’s VP of wearables, Alex Himel, talk fondly to the BBC about using his new smartglasses to film his daughter singing in a talent contest.
sentences.zh: 那次邂逅已经是不久前的事了，但上周，我听到 Meta 的可穿戴设备副总裁 Alex Himel 的一段视频，他深情地向 BBC 讲述了如何使用他的新智能眼镜拍摄他女儿在才艺比赛中唱歌的视频，这让我记忆犹新。

sentences.en: They let him capture the memory without having to watch her through a screen, he said, freeing him to be “kind of living in the moment, head up and hands free”.
sentences.zh: 他说，他们让他无需通过屏幕观看她就能捕捉到记忆，这让他“活在当下，抬起头，解放双手”。

sentences.en: So far, so sweet.
sentences.zh: 到目前为止，还是那么甜蜜。

sentences.en: But what about the risk of creeps potentially using them to film other people’s daughters covertly, heads up and hands free?
sentences.zh: 但是，那些小人可能会利用它们来偷偷地拍摄别人的女儿，抬起头来解放双手，这样的风险又如何呢？

sentences.en: Meta’s smartglasses have a tiny flashing warning light on the frame to indicate when the wearer is recording, but the technology is still new enough that many people don’t think to look for it.
sentences.zh: Meta 的智能眼镜镜框上有一个微小的闪烁警告灯，用于指示佩戴者何时进行录制，但这项技术仍然很新，以至于很多人都不会去寻找它。

sentences.en: And in the age of AI, stolen images of children matter.
sentences.zh: 在人工智能时代，被盗的儿童图像很重要。

sentences.en: Taken in real life or scraped from the internet, they’re the raw material for generating realistic-looking child abuse material of the most extreme and disturbing kind.
sentences.zh: 它们取自现实生活或从互联网上抓取，是生成最极端、最令人不安的真实虐待儿童材料的原材料。

sentences.en: (If that sounds like a grim but ultimately fairly victimless crime, the Internet Watch Foundation warned in a recent report that AI-generated material risks fuelling sexual interest in children, normalising violence and increasing the risk of real-life offending.)
sentences.zh: （互联网观察基金会在最近的一份报告中警告说，如果这听起来像是一种残酷但最终相当无受害者的犯罪，那么人工智能生成的材料可能会激发儿童的性兴趣，使暴力正常化并增加现实生活中犯罪的风险。）

But big tech hasn’t given up on the dream of connecting humans more seamlessly to the internet, and newer versions learn from past mistakes. Meta’s are cooler, featuring collaborations with Ray-Ban and Kylie Jenner, and are pitched unashamedly at parents under the wholesome slogan: “never miss a moment”. It’s an appealing sell to busy, guilty, multi-tasking parents. But the safeguarding concerns around covert filming are real enough for many nurseries and schools to have started banning them from the premises.

sentences.en: When Google launched the first smartglasses in 2013, they didn’t catch on: too nerdy-looking but also potentially too creepy, given the possibilities they afforded stalkers, trolls, criminals gathering intelligence and law enforcement in repressive regimes.
sentences.zh: 当谷歌在 2013 年推出第一款智能眼镜时，它们并没有流行起来：看起来太书呆子，但也可能太令人毛骨悚然，因为它们有可能为跟踪者、网络喷子、犯罪分子在专制政权中收集情报和执法提供帮助。

sentences.en: But big tech hasn’t given up on the dream of connecting humans more seamlessly to the internet, and newer versions learn from past mistakes.
sentences.zh: 但大型科技公司并没有放弃将人类更无缝地连接到互联网的梦想，新版本也从过去的错误中吸取了教训。

sentences.en: Meta’s are cooler, featuring collaborations with Ray-Ban and Kylie Jenner, and are pitched unashamedly at parents under the wholesome slogan: “never miss a moment”.
sentences.zh: Meta 的款式更酷，与雷朋 (Ray-Ban) 和凯莉·詹纳 (Kylie Jenner) 合作，并且毫不掩饰地向家长推销健康的口号：“不错过任何一个时刻”。

sentences.en: Want to capture your baby’s milestones, or do all the things you surreptitiously do when you’re with the kids – checking emails, shopping online, listening to podcasts – without taking your eyes off them?
sentences.zh: 想要捕捉宝宝的里程碑，或者做你和孩子们在一起时偷偷做的所有事情——检查电子邮件、网上购物、听播客——而不把眼睛从它们身上移开？

sentences.en: It’s an appealing sell to busy, guilty, multi-tasking parents.
sentences.zh: 对于忙碌、内疚、身兼多职的父母来说，这是一个很有吸引力的产品。

sentences.en: But the safeguarding concerns around covert filming are real enough for many nurseries and schools to have started banning them from the premises.
sentences.zh: 但围绕秘密拍摄的安全问题确实存在，许多托儿所和学校已经开始禁止他们进入场所。

blocks.html_en: A twist in the tale, however, is that making them socially unacceptable risks creating casualties of its own: the technology is potentially life-changing for blind people, helping them navigate confidently around obstacles or instantly translate text to audio.

sentences.en: A twist in the tale, however, is that making them socially unacceptable risks creating casualties of its own: the technology is potentially life-changing for blind people, helping them navigate confidently around obstacles or instantly translate text to audio.
sentences.zh: 然而，故事的一个转折是，让他们在社会上不可接受的风险会造成自己的伤亡：这项技术可能会改变盲人的生活，帮助他们自信地绕过障碍或立即将文本翻译成音频。

blocks.html_en: Meta has argued that it is for individuals to ensure they don’t “actively exploit” the technology, which makes you wonder if it learned anything at all from watching humans get to grips with its social media platforms. Regulatory options might include new privacy legislation designed for the digital age, forcing manufacturers to make it far clearer when the glasses are recording – maybe even with an audible warning, like the beep of a reversing lorry – at least until we’re all primed to look twice at anyone in specs. But since that would make them insufferably embarrassing to wear, it’s unlikely to please tech companies seeking a lucrative mass market.

sentences.en: Meta has argued that it is for individuals to ensure they don’t “actively exploit” the technology, which makes you wonder if it learned anything at all from watching humans get to grips with its social media platforms.
sentences.zh: Meta 认为，个人应该确保他们不会“积极利用”这项技术，这让你怀疑它是否从观察人类如何掌握其社交媒体平台中学到了任何东西。

sentences.en: Regulatory options might include new privacy legislation designed for the digital age, forcing manufacturers to make it far clearer when the glasses are recording – maybe even with an audible warning, like the beep of a reversing lorry – at least until we’re all primed to look twice at anyone in specs.
sentences.zh: 监管选项可能包括专为数字时代设计的新隐私立法，迫使制造商在眼镜录制时使其更加清晰——甚至可能发出声音警告，例如倒车卡车的蜂鸣声——至少直到我们都准备好仔细查看规格中的任何人为止。

sentences.en: But since that would make them insufferably embarrassing to wear, it’s unlikely to please tech companies seeking a lucrative mass market.
sentences.zh: 但由于这会让它们穿着起来非常尴尬，因此不太可能让寻求利润丰厚的大众市场的科技公司满意。

blocks.html_en: Another option could be holding manufacturers, not wearers, liable for malicious usage – though that could mean years of painfully slow litigation, concluding long after the damage is done. The nuclear option, meanwhile, would be a before-market regulatory regime for novel technology recognising its capacity for social harm, much as we have for clearing new pharmaceutical drugs or novel foodstuffs.

sentences.en: Another option could be holding manufacturers, not wearers, liable for malicious usage – though that could mean years of painfully slow litigation, concluding long after the damage is done.
sentences.zh: 另一种选择可能是让制造商而不是佩戴者对恶意使用承担责任——尽管这可能意味着多年痛苦而缓慢的诉讼，在损害造成很久之后才会得出结论。

sentences.en: The nuclear option, meanwhile, would be a before-market regulatory regime for novel technology recognising its capacity for social harm, much as we have for clearing new pharmaceutical drugs or novel foodstuffs.
sentences.zh: 与此同时，核选项将是新技术的上市前监管制度，承认其造成社会危害的能力，就像我们对新药品或新食品进行审批一样。

blocks.html_en: The best option is arguably the one history suggests is least likely to happen: for a myopic tech industry to start better anticipating, and designing out, potential harms that should be visible from space. There are some things, surely, that nobody needs smartglasses to see.

sentences.en: The best option is arguably the one history suggests is least likely to happen: for a myopic tech industry to start better anticipating, and designing out, potential harms that should be visible from space.
sentences.zh: 最好的选择可以说是历史表明最不可能发生的一种选择：对于短视的科技行业来说，开始更好地预测和设计从太空中可见的潜在危害。

sentences.en: There are some things, surely, that nobody needs smartglasses to see.
sentences.zh: 当然，有些事情没有人需要智能眼镜才能看到。

sentences.en: Gaby Hinsliff is a Guardian columnist
sentences.zh: 盖比·欣斯利夫 (Gaby Hinsliff) 是《卫报》专栏作家

sentences.en: Gaby Hinsliff is a Guardian columnist
sentences.zh: 盖比·欣斯利夫 (Gaby Hinsliff) 是《卫报》专栏作家

sentences.zh: 时事通讯促销后

content_html_zh:
我花了一段时间才意识到火车上坐在我旁边的那个人在做什么。 他正在手机上翻看一个小女孩的照片；放大一些图像，裁剪图像。 当然，这并不罕见，只是仔细观察，这些照片非常清楚地显示那个小孩坐在我们对面，和她的父母一起玩耍。 在拥挤的火车上，大多数人都在不知不觉地盯着自己的手机，一个陌生人偷偷地一遍又一遍地拍摄那个小女孩。 如果不是那个非常明显的手机屏幕暴露了他的身份，他就不会被抓住。

那次邂逅已经是不久前的事了，但上周，我听到 Meta 的可穿戴设备副总裁 Alex Himel 的一段视频，他深情地向 BBC 讲述了如何使用他的新智能眼镜拍摄他女儿在才艺比赛中唱歌的视频，这让我记忆犹新。 他说，他们让他无需通过屏幕观看她就能捕捉到记忆，这让他“活在当下，抬起头，解放双手”。 到目前为止，还是那么甜蜜。 但是，那些小人可能会利用它们来偷偷地拍摄别人的女儿，抬起头来解放双手，这样的风险又如何呢？ Meta 的智能眼镜镜框上有一个微小的闪烁警告灯，用于指示佩戴者何时进行录制，但这项技术仍然很新，以至于很多人都不会去寻找它。 在人工智能时代，被盗的儿童图像很重要。 它们取自现实生活或从互联网上抓取，是生成最极端、最令人不安的真实虐待儿童材料的原材料。 （互联网观察基金会在最近的一份报告中警告说，如果这听起来像是一种残酷但最终相当无受害者的犯罪，那么人工智能生成的材料可能会激发儿童的性兴趣，使暴力正常化并增加现实生活中犯罪的风险。）

当谷歌在 2013 年推出第一款智能眼镜时，它们并没有流行起来：看起来太书呆子，但也可能太令人毛骨悚然，因为它们有可能为跟踪者、网络喷子、犯罪分子在专制政权中收集情报和执法提供帮助。 但大型科技公司并没有放弃将人类更无缝地连接到互联网的梦想，新版本也从过去的错误中吸取了教训。 Meta 的款式更酷，与雷朋 (Ray-Ban) 和凯莉·詹纳 (Kylie Jenner) 合作，并且毫不掩饰地向家长推销健康的口号：“不错过任何一个时刻”。 想要捕捉宝宝的里程碑，或者做你和孩子们在一起时偷偷做的所有事情——检查电子邮件、网上购物、听播客——而不把眼睛从它们身上移开？ 对于忙碌、内疚、身兼多职的父母来说，这是一个很有吸引力的产品。 但围绕秘密拍摄的安全问题确实存在，许多托儿所和学校已经开始禁止他们进入场所。

然而，故事的一个转折是，让他们在社会上不可接受的风险会造成自己的伤亡：这项技术可能会改变盲人的生活，帮助他们自信地绕过障碍或立即将文本翻译成音频。

Meta 认为，个人应该确保他们不会“积极利用”这项技术，这让你怀疑它是否从观察人类如何掌握其社交媒体平台中学到了任何东西。 监管选项可能包括专为数字时代设计的新隐私立法，迫使制造商在眼镜录制时使其更加清晰——甚至可能发出声音警告，例如倒车卡车的蜂鸣声——至少直到我们都准备好仔细查看规格中的任何人为止。 但由于这会让它们穿着起来非常尴尬，因此不太可能让寻求利润丰厚的大众市场的科技公司满意。

另一种选择可能是让制造商而不是佩戴者对恶意使用承担责任——尽管这可能意味着多年痛苦而缓慢的诉讼，在损害造成很久之后才会得出结论。 与此同时，核选项将是新技术的上市前监管制度，承认其造成社会危害的能力，就像我们对新药品或新食品进行审批一样。

最好的选择可以说是历史表明最不可能发生的一种选择：对于短视的科技行业来说，开始更好地预测和设计从太空中可见的潜在危害。 当然，有些事情没有人需要智能眼镜才能看到。

  • 盖比·欣斯利夫 (Gaby Hinsliff) 是《卫报》专栏作家
